_note: Tournament storylines. Update status as the tournament progresses: pre-tournament → building → climax → resolved.

narratives.title: Messi's Last Stand
narratives.summary: Lionel Messi arrives at the 2026 World Cup as the reigning champion and almost certainly in his final tournament. At 38, he carries the weight of a nation's love and the question every soccer fan is asking: can he do it twice?
narratives.watchFor: How does Argentina manage Messi's minutes? Does he show up in the knockout rounds when it matters most? And what does his face look like if they go out early?

narratives.title: Mbappé Unchained
narratives.summary: Kylian Mbappé has won everything — except the World Cup as the undisputed best player in the world. In 2022 he scored a hat trick in the final and still lost. In 2026 he arrives at the peak of his powers with France looking like one of the two or three best teams in the tournament.
narratives.watchFor: Does Mbappé finally convert a major tournament into a winner's medal? And what's the subplot between him and anyone France has to face in the knockouts?

narratives.title: USMNT on Home Soil
narratives.summary: The United States has never won a World Cup, and they've never hosted one with a team this talented. Playing in front of massive home crowds — including in LA, Dallas, and New York — the USMNT has a generational chance to turn the country into a soccer nation overnight.
narratives.watchFor: How far do they go? Does the home crowd energy translate to results? And how does the country react if they make a deep run — or crash out early?

narratives.title: Brazil's 24-Year Drought
narratives.summary: Brazil last won the World Cup in 2002 in Japan and South Korea. Since then: quarterfinal exits, a 7-1 humiliation at home in 2014, and years of near-misses. In 2026 they arrive with Vinicius Jr, Endrick, and a squad that has been building toward this for years.
narratives.watchFor: Can they shake the psychological weight of 24 years? Does Vinicius Jr announce himself as the world's best player? And can Brazil handle a knockout-stage pressure situation?

narratives.title: Mexico Breaks the Curse
narratives.summary: Mexico has been eliminated in the Round of 16 in every World Cup from 1994 to 2018 — eight straight times. As co-hosts in 2026, playing an opening match at the sacred Estadio Azteca, they have the most emotionally charged chance in decades to finally break through.
narratives.watchFor: Do they get past the Round of 16 for the first time in 32 years? The Azteca crowd will be deafening. And can a new generation of Mexican players carry that weight?

narratives.title: South Africa: The Opening Match Shock
narratives.summary: South Africa is handed the most difficult opening match in World Cup history: Mexico at the Azteca on opening day. But upsets happen. South Africa's 2010 home World Cup showed what belief and occasion can do. An opening-day shock would be one of the great moments in tournament history.
narratives.watchFor: Can South Africa survive the Azteca atmosphere and grind out a result? Even a draw would be historic. A win would be one of the best moments of the tournament.

narratives.title: Morocco: The New African Giant
narratives.summary: Morocco reached the World Cup semifinal in 2022 — the first African nation ever to do so. Their run electrified the continent and changed what people believe African teams can achieve. In 2026, they return as genuine contenders, not underdogs.
narratives.watchFor: Can they go further? Can they win it? And does their success inspire a generation of players from Africa and the Arab world to believe bigger?

narratives.title: England: Is It Coming Home?
narratives.summary: England reached the Euro 2020 final and Euro 2024 final. They have arguably the most talented squad in English history. But the weight of 60 years without a major trophy still sits heavy. The 2026 World Cup is perhaps the peak opportunity for this generation.
narratives.watchFor: Do they finally close out a major tournament? Or do they find yet another heartbreaking way to fall just short? The English football psyche is watching.

narratives.title: Canada's Home Tournament Debut
narratives.summary: Canada qualified for their first World Cup in 36 years in 2022 and performed above expectations. Now as co-hosts in 2026, playing at BMO Field in Toronto in front of their own country, they arrive with a generation of players that could make Canadians care about soccer the way they care about hockey.
narratives.watchFor: How does Canada respond to the pressure of being a host? Does Alphonso Davies put on a show? And can they advance further than they ever have?

narratives.title: The 48-Team Experiment
narratives.summary: For the first time, the World Cup features 48 teams and 104 matches. Critics say it dilutes the competition. Supporters say it expands access, creates more upsets, and gives smaller nations a seat at the table. 2026 will answer the question.
narratives.watchFor: Do the extra teams create great stories or just forgettable group stage padding? Does a true minnow shock a giant? The format is on trial.

narratives.title: Haaland's World Cup Arrival
narratives.summary: Erling Haaland is 25 years old, the best striker on the planet, and this is his first senior World Cup. Norway are in Group I alongside France. The defining image of the 2026 tournament may be Haaland scoring against the defending runners-up. This is what everyone is waiting for.
narratives.watchFor: Does Haaland finally get to show the world what he can do in a World Cup? Can Norway — a nation that has never reached a World Cup final — use him as a launching pad to go further than anyone expects?

narratives.title: Bosnia-Herzegovina: A Nation's First World Cup
narratives.summary: Bosnia-Herzegovina are playing their first World Cup since 2014 — and their match against Qatar at Lumen Field in Seattle on June 24 is the official start of their campaign. A nation that only declared independence in 1992 is back on the world's biggest stage. June 24 in Seattle will be one of the most emotional sporting moments in Bosnian history.
narratives.watchFor: The Bosnian diaspora in Seattle is real. Watch for fans from every corner of the city converging on Lumen Field to watch their nation play in the city they now call home.
